name: Swan Maiden
description: Swan maidens are mythical beings, often depicted as women who possess the ability to transform into swans. They appear in various forms of Germanic folklore, most notably in Norse mythology and related Germanic traditions. Typically, they are beautiful and ethereal creatures who shed their swan skins to bathe in secluded lakes or ponds, revealing their human forms. A common motif involves a mortal man stealing or hiding the swan skin, thus forcing the swan maiden to remain in human form and often marry him. However, she always retains a longing for her former life and, upon recovering her swan skin, will inevitably return to her avian existence, leaving her human husband behind. The stories often explore themes of freedom, captivity, and the clash between the mortal and supernatural realms. These figures are associated with grace, beauty, and a connection to the natural world, particularly bodies of water.
subtitle: A woman who can transform into a swan.
appearance: In swan form, they appear as pristine white swans, often larger and more graceful than ordinary birds. In human form, they are exceptionally beautiful women, often with long flowing hair, fair skin, and an ethereal quality. They may retain subtle avian features, such as exceptionally keen eyesight or a preference for aquatic environments.
habitat: Secluded lakes, ponds, rivers, and other bodies of water, often in remote or forested areas. They are also sometimes associated with the realm of the gods or other supernatural realms.
behavior: While in swan form, they are graceful and elusive. In human form, they are often shy and reserved, but also possess a captivating allure. They are drawn to solitude and natural beauty. Their behavior is often driven by a longing for their true home and a desire for freedom.

keyMyths.title: The Legend of Wayland the Smith
keyMyths.description: In the Völundarkviða (Lay of Wayland) from the Poetic Edda, Wayland the Smith marries a Valkyrie, Hervör alvitr. She and her sisters are described as swan maidens. After nine years, they fly away, leaving Wayland heartbroken. This story highlights the fleeting nature of their connection to the human world and their eventual return to their supernatural origins.

keyMyths.title: The Swan Knight
keyMyths.description: Several versions of the Swan Knight legend exist, including the story of Lohengrin. He arrives in a boat pulled by a swan, helps a damsel in distress, and marries her. However, he stipulates that she must never ask him his name or origin. When she eventually breaks this promise, the swan returns, and Lohengrin is forced to leave, revealing his divine heritage.

keyMyths.title: The Seven Swan Brothers
keyMyths.description: This is a common folktale motif where siblings are transformed into swans, often through a curse or spell. A sister or other female relative must then undertake a difficult quest to break the enchantment and restore them to their human forms. This tale explores themes of sacrifice, perseverance, and the power of familial love.
keyMyths.source: Brothers Grimm, The Seven Ravens (related tale)

sources.author: Davidson, H.R. Ellis

sources.description: Provides a comprehensive overview of Norse mythology, including discussions of various supernatural beings and their roles in the sagas.
